EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong area of low pressure tracked from southwestern Indiana to the northern Great Lakes during the morning and afternoon hours on April 16th. As the area of low pressure entered Michigan and the northern Great Lakes, strong northerly winds hit northern Ohio. Along with the high winds, showers and thunderstorms ahead of the cold front produced hail across parts of northern Ohio. As the deep area of low pressure continued to move northeast into Canada, high winds continued across northern Ohio through the morning hours of the 17th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds downed a few large tree branches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of very strong low pressure moved northeast across the Great Lakes during the evening hours of April 27th and the morning hours of the 28th.  A strong cold front stretching south from this low swept east across northern Ohio prior to daybreak on the 28th.  Following the passage of the cold front, strong northwesterly winds developed over northern Ohio.  Peak winds at most locations occurred during the predawn hours of the 28th.  Winds gusted to as much as 70 mph in some areas and much damage was reported.  Most of the damage was from downed trees, power lines and utility poles.  Hundreds of homes also sustained damage, mainly from lost roofing or siding.  A semi truck was blown over in Marion County.  Scattered power outages were also reported across the area with over 100,000 homes and businesses affected.  Peak wind gusts measured by automated sensors in northern Ohio included:  69 mph at the Lorain County Airport; 68 mph at the Findlay Airport in Hancock County; 67 mph at Burke Lakefront Airport in Cuyahoga County; 63 mph at Mansfield Lahm Airport in Richland County; 61 mph at Cleveland Hopkins International Airport in Cuyahoga County; 61 mph at the Akron-Canton Regional Airport in Summit County; 60 mph at Metcalf Field in Wood County; 58 mph at the Marion County Airport and 58 mph at the Wayne County Airport.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust of 62 mph was measured during this event. Numerous power outages were also reported at this time.  Many homes sustained damage, mainly from lost roofing or siding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Repetitive showers and thunderstorms, moved southeast through western Vinton County, Jackson County, and western and central portions of Lawrence County between 1500E and 1830E on Tuesday, the 10th.  This convection was just northeast of the surface warm front.  A sharp dew point gradient existed along the front.  Surface dew points were around 70 degrees just southwest of the boundary.  Luckily, later that same evening, repetitive convection occurred in the Scioto River Valley, missing this area just to the west.   ||Initially, the main impact was large hail.  As back building caused repetitive showers and thunderstorms, flooding became the primary issue.  Maximum rain amounts of 3 to 4 inches were observed.  Jackson measured 3.35 inches.  Waterloo observed 3.76 inches.  South Point had 3.25 inches of rain.||Luckily, no injuries or fatalities occurred. ||State assistance money was committed to aid uninsured homeowners and renters.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the evening and nighttime hours of May 11th, an area of low pressure located over southern Wisconsin slowly progressed northeast over the upper Great Lakes. A frontal boundary extended southeast of the low, across portions of Indiana into southern Ohio. That frontal boundary lifted north during the day on May 12 and stalled directly over northern Ohio. Thunderstorms developed along this boundary and produced large hail and significant rainfall throughout the day on May 12th. The severe thunderstorms and flash flooding continued through May 13th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced a swath of pea to quarter sized hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system west of the Ohio Valley approached during peak heating on May 25th. This resulted in the development of widespread severe thunderstorms with large hail and damaging winds during the afternoon and evening hours. Later in the evening, a quasi-linear convective system swept west to east across the area. The result was straight line wind damage and a few tornadoes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Between May 25th and May 26th heavy rainfall occurred as a warm front lifted north across the area. A strong southerly flow, ahead of a cold front, transported very moist air through Tennessee, Kentucky and into Ohio on the 25th. Surface dew points were in the 60s. Wave after wave in the mid and upper levels helped trigger widespread showers, with embedded thunderstorms. High rainfall rates of over an inch per hour occured with the stronger storms, resulting in widespread flood warnings across Northern Ohio. The showers continued into the evening hours, and then reformed early on the 26th. By this point the ground was saturated and streams and creeks were running high. Additional storms compounded lingering flooding conditions, necessitating a second round of widespread flood warnings. Numerous state and local routes in the area were closed because of the quickly rising and flowing water.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced a measured wind gust of 60 mph.

Thunderstorms developed over southern West Virginia and eastern Kentucky during the heat and humidity of this June afternoon.  The storms moved northeast into southeast Ohio by the early evening.  Despite the high freezing level, some large hail still occurred.  The rapid temperature drop from near 90 to 70 degrees also helped produced some damaging wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck a nursing home, damaging its roof and causing a small electrical fire.  Through the smoke and the water pouring down from the sprinklers, nurses moved several inhabitants to another wing of the building or outside.  A few residents were carried through an open window.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the afternoon on the 23rd, a broken line of convection formed in southeast Ohio ahead of a slow moving cold front.  Dry air in the mid levels, as well as deep layer undirectional shear, lent toward a damaging wind threat as opposed to large hail. ||After the initial line passed well into West Virginia, recovering surface temperatures and enough shear was present to support further development during the early evening.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed over southern West Virginia and eastern Kentucky during the heat and humidity of this June afternoon.  The storms moved northeast into southeast Ohio by the early evening.  Despite the high freezing level, some large hail still occurred.  The rapid temperature drop from near 90 to 70 degrees also helped produced some damaging wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down, blocking Route 52.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple thunderstorms move over Lorain County between the 25th and 26th of May. The storms on the 25th saturated the ground, allowing for rapid runoff from storms on the 26th. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Between May 25th and May 26th heavy rainfall occurred as a warm front lifted north across the area. A strong southerly flow, ahead of a cold front, transported very moist air through Tennessee, Kentucky and into Ohio on the 25th. Surface dew points were in the 60s. Wave after wave in the mid and upper levels helped trigger widespread showers, with embedded thunderstorms. High rainfall rates of over an inch per hour occured with the stronger storms, resulting in widespread flood warnings across Northern Ohio. Numerous state and local routes in the area were closed because of the quickly rising and flowing water.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter sized hail was reported. Thunderstorms continued to produce nickel size hail for several minutes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the afternoon on the 23rd, a broken line of convection formed in southeast Ohio ahead of a slow moving cold front.  Dry air in the mid levels, as well as deep layer undirectional shear, lent toward a damaging wind threat as opposed to large hail. ||After the initial line passed well into West Virginia, recovering surface temperatures and enough shear was present to support further development during the early evening.

Thunderstorms developed over southern West Virginia and eastern Kentucky during the heat and humidity of this June afternoon.  The storms moved northeast into southeast Ohio by the early evening.  Despite the high freezing level, some large hail still occurred.  The rapid temperature drop from near 90 to 70 degrees also helped produced some damaging wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large section of metal roof to a business was ripped off.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system west of the Ohio Valley approached during peak heating on May 25th. This resulted in the development of widespread severe thunderstorms with large hail and damaging winds during the afternoon and evening hours. Later in the evening, a quasi-linear convective system swept west to east across the area. The result was straight line wind damage and a few tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There was damage to a barn and a roof removed from a garage due to damaging thunderstorm winds.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A supercell thunderstorm moved across Hancock County.  Hail as large as baseballs fell in association with this thunderstorm.  The western half of the city of Findlay was especially hard hit.  Golf ball or larger hail affected an area bordered by State Route 12 to the south, Township Road 99 to the north, County Road 140 to the west and roughly Main Street to the east.  As many as 4,000 homes and business in this area may have been damaged by the hail.  Thousands of automobiles also sustained damage from the hail.  One automobile dealership on the north side of Findlay had over 400 vehicles damaged by the hail.  Hundreds of homes also sustained damage from broken windows or damaged siding.  This event could end up being one of costliest hail storms in Ohio history.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure moved up from the Mississippi River Valley into Ohio. The storm system had adequate moisture from the Gulf of Mexico, which increased rainfall rates to over an inch and a half per hour. This intense rainfall triggered several small rivers and streams to overflow their banks, and for low lying and poor drainage areas to flood. The highest rainfall amounts were observed over northwest OH with 2 to 3 inches reported near Toledo. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Torrential rain from passing thunderstorms brought as much as 2.5 or rain to portions of Cuyahoga County. This intense rainfall over saturated grounds produced rapid runoff. The result was several roads flooded, including Smith Road in Middleburg Heights where several inches of flowing water was observed. Portions of Westlake, Parma, and Garfield Heights experienced flooding in non-typical places as well. West 130th St in Cleveland Ohio was impassable due to flash flooding when several feet of flowing water filled the road in 10 minutes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system west of the Ohio Valley approached during peak heating on May 25th. This resulted in the development of widespread severe thunderstorms with large hail and damaging winds during the afternoon and evening hours. Later in the evening, a quasi-linear convective system swept west to east across the area. The result was straight line wind damage and a few tornadoes.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF1 tornado touched down in a cemetery on the southeast side of Monroeville.  This tornado then continued east on the ground for about eight miles before lifting near the intersection of Tiger Road and North Greenwich Milan Road on the southeast side of Norwalk.  The path was parallel and just south of U.S. Route 20 for several miles.  Around a dozen homes and businesses were damaged by this tornado.  A couple of the buildings lost large sections of roofing with some of damage caused by fallen trees.  The tornado also leveled a large barn as it crossed Halfway Road.   Several dozen trees were also downed along the damage path which was no more than 50 yards in width.

A strong southerly flow, ahead of a cold front, transported very moist air through Tennessee, Kentucky and into Ohio on the 25th. Surface dew points were in the 60s. Wave after wave in the mid and upper levels helped trigger widespread showers, with embedded thunderstorms. High rainfall rates of over an inch per hour occured with the stronger storms, resulting in widespread flood warnings across Northern Ohio. The showers continued into the evening hours, and then reformed early on the 26th. By this point the ground was saturated and streams and creeks were running high. Additional storms compounded lingering flooding conditions, necessitating a second round of widespread flood warnings. Numerous state and local routes in the area were closed because of the quickly rising and flowing water.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced a swath of nickel to quarter size hail as they tracked across the county. Locations impacted by the hail included Westlake, Rocky River, and Lakewood.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the evening and nighttime hours of May 11th, an area of low pressure located over southern Wisconsin slowly progressed northeast over the upper Great Lakes. A frontal boundary extended southeast of the low, across portions of Indiana into southern Ohio. That frontal boundary lifted north during the day on May 12 and stalled directly over northern Ohio. Thunderstorms developed along this boundary and produced large hail and significant rainfall throughout the day on May 12th. The severe thunderstorms and flash flooding continued through May 13th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced a swath of quarter to golf ball sized hail. Numerous vehicles were damaged by hail. Homes and crops were also damaged as a result of the large hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure moved up from the Mississippi River Valley into Ohio. The storm system had adequate moisture from the Gulf of Mexico, which increased rainfall rates to over an inch and a half per hour. This intense rainfall triggered several small rivers and streams to overflow their banks, and for low lying and poor drainage areas to flood. The highest rainfall amounts were observed over northwest OH with 2 to 3 inches reported near Toledo. Amounts this high were observed elsewhere, though they were more spotty and not over the same basins. Widespread 1 to 2 inches was the average rainfall totals across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Torrential rain fell over Trumbull County on the evening of the 14th, this after three preceding days of flooding. The saturated ground could not handle additional rainfall, so the one to two inches that fell on this evenig resulted in flash flooding. Several creeks mainly in northern portions of the county came out of their banks. Basements flooded in the Howland Township area. Several roads were impassable including OH 193.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large area of rain and embedded thunderstorms moved from central into northern Ohio during the afternoon and early evening hours as  warm front lifted north over Lake Erie. Strong low level winds and an upper level low supported a threat for severe weather, with a tornado watched issued in the afternoon.  The strong jet coming out of the gulf funneled moist air into the region, supporting very efficient rain. Rainfall reports as high as of 3 inches in 2 hours were observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Training thunderstorms with torrential showers brought as much as 2 to 3 inches of rain over the Berlin Heights region. This heavy rainfall fell during rush hour and all within 2 hours. This resulted in several flooded roads with water as high as bumpers, and several road birms were washed away.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado blew down two high voltage towers along the Taylor Pike Road and destroyed a metal barn. The debris from the barn was deposited 300 to 400 feet across a field and into a tree line. A couple pieces of debris from the frame of the barn were impaled into the field. There were also some snapped trees and branches down along the tree line. The maximum estimated wind speed based on damage was 85 miles per hour.

A strong low pressure system west of the Ohio Valley approached during peak heating on May 25th. This resulted in the development of widespread severe thunderstorms with large hail and damaging winds during the afternoon and evening hours. Later in the evening, a quasi-linear convective system swept west to east across the area. The result was straight line wind damage and a few tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and power lines were reported down due to damaging thunderstorm winds.

Morning showers and thunderstorms in northern Ohio dropped south into southeast Ohio during the afternoon.  Strong heating helped increase buoyancy during the afternoon.

A cold front moved into Northern and Central Ohio during the day...then became stationary. Ground conditions were saturated from recent rainfall, so the additional storms and showers that developed along this boundary resulted in flooding. Some moist air from the Gulf of Mexico supplied sufficient moisture for rainfall rates over an inch per hour, which allowed for rapid runoff. Storm total rainfall amounts were generally an inch to an inch and a half along a line from Findlay, to Mansfield, and east to Youngstown. Though not a signficant amount of rain, it was sufficient to bring small streams and creeks out of their banks and lead to flooding of low lying and poor drainage areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms along a stationary front brought around 1.5 of rainfall to Portage County. Recent rain prevented the ground from absorbing much if any of this rain, resulting in almost complete runoff. Several streets were inundated with as much as a foot of water, and the Atwater Water Treatment Plant experienced flooding.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain falling over an inch per hour hit Lorain during rush hour. Several roads were flooded in and around Lorain, with the worse hit roads on West 23rd St, West 43rd St, and Georgia Avenue. The water was up to the car doors in some spots. One person had to abandon their vehicle on West 23rd St.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Between May 25th and May 26th heavy rainfall occurred as a warm front lifted north across the area. A strong southerly flow, ahead of a cold front, transported very moist air through Tennessee, Kentucky and into Ohio on the 25th. Surface dew points were in the 60s. Wave after wave in the mid and upper levels helped trigger widespread showers, with embedded thunderstorms. High rainfall rates of over an inch per hour occured with the stronger storms, resulting in widespread flood warnings across Northern Ohio. The showers continued into the evening hours, and then reformed early on the 26th. By this point the ground was saturated and streams and creeks were running high. Additional storms compounded lingering flooding conditions, necessitating a second round of widespread flood warnings. Numerous state and local routes in the area were closed because of the quickly rising and flowing water.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds downed two large trees.

During the evening and nighttime hours of May 11th, an area of low pressure located over southern Wisconsin slowly progressed northeast over the upper Great Lakes. A frontal boundary extended southeast of the low, across portions of Indiana into southern Ohio. That frontal boundary lifted north during the day on May 12 and stalled directly over northern Ohio. Thunderstorms developed along this boundary and produced large hail and significant rainfall throughout the day on May 12th. The severe thunderstorms and flash flooding continued through May 13th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Torrential rain with rates over an inch per hour fell over northwest portions of Trumbull County. As much as 2 inches fell within an hour and a half. Several roads were flooded including Route 45. Water levels came up so fast in some local streams and creeks that farm animals had to be rescued in Farmington.

An upper level jet stream tracking through the Ohio Valley combined with low pressure at the surface to produce severe thunderstorms during the afternoon of March 23rd. Several supercells developed producing large hail.  There were also a few thunderstorm line segments with damaging wind gusts ahead of drier air rapidly advancing from the west. A second round of thunderstorms occurred closer to the main cold front during the evening of March 23rd to just after midnight on March 24th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The wind gust was estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure moving along a stalled front turned mixed precipitation to heavy wet snow the afternoon of the 21st across eastern Ohio, western Pennsylvania, and the northern panhandle of West Virginia. The snow continued into the early morning of the 22nd. Snowfall rates up to 2 inches an hour produced storm totals of 6 to 10 inches across the region. Much of the snow fell during the evening rush hour from Steubenville, Ohio east to Pittsburgh and Greensburg, Pennsylvania bringing the evening commute to a standstill. Numerous cars were abandoned on roadways with some travelers spending nearly 8 hours to reach their destinations. Pockets of freezing rain were south of the area of heavy snow with more than one quarter of an inch of ice reported in Preston county West Virginia.

A strong low pressure system moved across the Upper Ohio Valley bringing heavy rain as well as a line of severe thunderstorms. Flooding of streams and creeks was widespread across eastern Ohio and western Pennsylvania. Isolated wind damage from severe thunderstorms occurred with a cold front in the morning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency management reported flooding countywide as most creeks out of their banks and flooding roadways.

A line of severe storms moved through during the morning hours of February 28th.  The main threats were damaging winds and flash flooding, however one tornado also occurred with this event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple homes were flooded with water coming into the windows.  Evacuations were also necessary across the flooded area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure tracked slowly east across the Ohio Valley on February 20th and 21st.  A warm front stretching east from this low moved north into northern Ohio on the 20th and caused freezing rain to develop from west to east during the evening hours.  The freezing rain continued for several hours and then ended just before daybreak on the 21st.  Ice accumulations of between one quarter and one third of an inch were reported from Lorain County eastward across the Cleveland metropolitan area and into Lake, Geauga and Trumbull Counties.  The freezing rain was much lighter further south and west.  Colder temperatures were reported in Ashtabula County which allowed most of the precipitation to fall as snow and resulted in little if any ice accumulation.  At the peak of the storm, over 80,000 people were without power in northern Ohio.  Mass transit systems in Cuyahoga County were briefly shut down because of power outages.  It took nearly a full day for the power outages to be completely restored.  Many schools not already closed for the President's Day Holiday either canceled or delayed classes on the 21st.  A second wave of precipitation affected northern Ohio during the afternoon and evening hours of the 21st.  This second round of precipitation fell entirely as snow.  The snow was heaviest along and north of the U.S. Highway 30 corridor with four to six hours of moderate to heavy snow reported.  Snowfall rates at times exceeded two inches per hour with visibilities near zero.  Over six inches of snow fell from Crawford County eastward across the Mansfield, Wooster, Canton and Youngstown areas.  The heavy snow also extended north into Medina, Summit and Portage Counties.  Accumulations of eight or more inches were reported in portions of northern Richland, Ashland and Wayne Counties.  The heaviest snow fell during the evening commute and travel was nearly impossible at times.  Motorists reported their commute times were several times normal.  Interstate 76 in Summit and Medina Counties was closed for nearly six hours after numerous accidents occurred between 5 and 6 pm.  Hundreds of motorists were stranded in their cars.   It took most of the night for road crews to clean the area roads up.  Several local officials reported that conditions during the peak of the storm were the worst they had seen in years.  Many school districts remained closed on the 22nd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure tracked slowly east across the Ohio Valley on February 20th and 21st. Snow associated with this system spread into Wayne County during the afternoon hours of the 21st. Four to six hours of moderate to heavy snow occurred from the middle portion of the afternoon through early evening.  Snowfall rates at times exceeded two inches per hour with visibilities near zero. Six to eight inches of snow fell over much of Wayne County.  It took most of the night for road crews to clean the area roads up. Several local officials reported that conditions during the peak of the storm were the worst they had seen in years. Many school districts were closed on the 22nd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moderate to heavy snow impacted much of northwest Ohio during the morning hours of February 25th as an area of low pressure tracked northeastward near the Ohio River. Snowfall amounts generally ranged between four and seven inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate to heavy snow developed during the early morning hours of February 25th and continued through approximately 9:00 am ET that morning. Snowfall rates near an inch per hour were reported. Snowfall totals across Allen County were generally between 6 and 7 inches, with a trained spotter in Spencerville reporting 6.5 inches of total snow accumulation. The heavy snow resulted in accidents and slide-offs across the region, along with school delays and closures.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Periods of freezing rain created hazardous driving conditions across northwest Ohio February 20th into the morning hours of February 21st as moisture lifted northward over a nearly stalled out frontal boundary. Ice accumulations generally ranged between a glaze and 0.30. The freezing rain changed over to a period of heavy snow during the afternoon and early evening hours of February 21st.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Freezing rain developed around noon on February 20th and continued through the rest of the day into the early morning hours of February 21st. Ice accretions in excess of 0.25 were common across the county. This created icing on power lines and trees which led to power outages across the county. There were also reports of slide-offs and accidents. There was a lull in the precipitation during the morning of February 21st. Moderate to heavy snow developed in the afternoon, continuing into the early evening and accumulating to between 3 and 5 inches. The snow and ice led to school closings and hazardous driving conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure developed over the southern Plains on February 1st and then moved quickly northeast.  This low moved across northern Ohio during the morning hours of February 2nd.  Three waves of wintry precipitation were associated with this storm across northern Ohio.  The first wave of precipitation moved into Northwest Ohio during the late evening hours of January 31st.  This precipitation overspread the remainder of northern Ohio during the early morning hours of the 1st.  Locations south a line from near Marion to Mansfield to Warren in Trumbull County saw a mixture of freezing rain and sleet.  Widespread ice accumulations of over a quarter inch were reported in this area with up to a half inch of ice accumulation in portions of Stark, Holmes and Mahoning Counties.  Locations further north saw a four to six hour period of moderate to heavy snow.  Snow accumulations from the Toledo area eastward across the Cleveland metropolitan area and into Northeast Ohio were between four and six inches with a few reports of up to seven inches.  Some sleet was also reported.  This first wave of precipitation exited Ohio to the east around daybreak on the 1st.  Most of the daylight hours of the 1st were quiet with just some light snow and patchy freezing drizzle reported.  The second wave of precipitation began during the late afternoon and evening hours.  Most of this was also mixed freezing rain and sleet.  Only the northwestern corner of the area saw predominantly snow.  Ice accumulations of between one quarter and one half inch were reported along with an inch or more of sleet accumulation.  This mixture coated area roads and streets and resulted in treacherous travel.  A few power outages resulted from the freezing rain early on the 1st.  Power outages were more numerous on 2nd.  Trees and power lines already weighted down from ice from the 1st gave way with this second round of ice.  Stark County was especially hard hit by power outages.  Over 50,000 residents in the Canton area alone lost power.  It took several days for power to be fully restored.  Wrap around snow behind the low affected northern Ohio during the daylight hours of the 2nd.  Some of the snow was locally heavy with visibilities at times less than a quarter mile.  Much of the area saw between one and three inches of snow.  The snow quickly ended from west to east during the evening hours.  Strong winds also accompanied the precipitation.  Winds were initially from the northeast and gusty.  Gusts up to 35 mph were reported near the Lake Erie shoreline.  Winds became southerly right ahead of the low and then became southwest to west as the low exited to the east.  Wind gusts as much as 40 mph occurred during the morning hours of the 2nd with speeds gradually subsiding during the afternoon.  A lot of blowing and drifting snow was reported.  Over 500 schools were closed in northern Ohio on February 1st and 2nd.  Many schools remained closed on February 3rd because of residual thick ice on parking lots, sidewalks and secondary roads.  This ice resulted from the combination of two days of mixed freezing rain, sleet and snow and was up to two inches thick in some areas.  Removing the ice from sidewalks and driveways was nearly impossible.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure moved up the Ohio Valley on January 11th and crossed northern Ohio during the evening hours.  Snow associated with this low began around midday on the 11th with periods of moderate to heavy snow reported during the afternoon.  The snow lessened for a time during the evening but picked up again after midnight on the 12th as cold northerly winds behind the low began to interact with Lake Erie.  More moderate to heavy snow occurred during the early morning hours of the 12th.  The snow showers finally tapered to flurries late in the afternoon.  Snowfall totals across Cuyahoga, Summit and Geauga Counties ranged from 6 to 12 inches.  Peak totals for these counties included: 15.4 inches at Sagamore Hills in Summit County; 14.0 inches in Russell Township in Geauga County and 12.7 inches at Broadview Heights in Cuyahoga County.  Northwest winds gusted to as much as 25 mph during this event and caused considerable blowing and drifting.  Travel was very difficult during this event.  Many school districts were either closed or delayed during this storm.  Many accidents were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense low pressure system tracked from the Lower Mississippi River Valley northeast into northern Indiana late February 1st into February 2nd. Heavy snow and sleet developed out ahead of this system during the late afternoon and evening hours of February 1st, with many locations picking up between 3 and 7 inches of snow and sleet. This combined with wind gusts in excess of 40 mph created blizzard conditions across much of northwest Ohio.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Blizzard conditions were experienced during the late afternoon and evening hours of February 1st as an intense low pressure system approached from the southwest. Wind gusts in excess of 40 mph, combined with the falling heavy snow and sleet, created reduced visibilities below an eighth of a mile at times. Snowfall rates of 1 to 2 inches per hour were common, with totals across the county generally ranging between 4 and 7 inches. Snow drifts of 2 to 3 feet were also reported across the county. The significant blowing and drifting snow resulted in numerous accidents and school closings across the region.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure tracked slowly east across the Ohio Valley on February 20th and 21st.  A warm front stretching east from this low moved north into northern Ohio on the 20th and caused freezing rain to develop from west to east during the evening hours.  The freezing rain continued for several hours and then ended just before daybreak on the 21st.  Ice accumulations of between one quarter and one third of an inch were reported from Lorain County eastward across the Cleveland metropolitan area and into Lake, Geauga and Trumbull Counties.  The freezing rain was much lighter further south and west.  Colder temperatures were reported in Ashtabula County which allowed most of the precipitation to fall as snow and resulted in little if any ice accumulation.  At the peak of the storm, over 80,000 people were without power in northern Ohio.  Mass transit systems in Cuyahoga County were briefly shut down because of power outages.  It took nearly a full day for the power outages to be completely restored.  Many schools not already closed for the President's Day Holiday either canceled or delayed classes on the 21st.  A second wave of precipitation affected northern Ohio during the afternoon and evening hours of the 21st.  This second round of precipitation fell entirely as snow.  The snow was heaviest along and north of the U.S. Highway 30 corridor with four to six hours of moderate to heavy snow reported.  Snowfall rates at times exceeded two inches per hour with visibilities near zero.  Over six inches of snow fell from Crawford County eastward across the Mansfield, Wooster, Canton and Youngstown areas.  The heavy snow also extended north into Medina, Summit and Portage Counties.  Accumulations of eight or more inches were reported in portions of northern Richland, Ashland and Wayne Counties.  The heaviest snow fell during the evening commute and travel was nearly impossible at times.  Motorists reported their commute times were several times normal.  Interstate 76 in Summit and Medina Counties was closed for nearly six hours after numerous accidents occurred between 5 and 6 pm.  Hundreds of motorists were stranded in their cars.   It took most of the night for road crews to clean the area roads up.  Several local officials reported that conditions during the peak of the storm were the worst they had seen in years.  Many school districts remained closed on the 22nd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense low pressure system tracked from the Lower Mississippi River Valley northeast into northern Indiana late February 1st into February 2nd. Heavy snow and sleet developed out ahead of this system during the late afternoon and evening hours of February 1st, with many locations picking up between 3 and 7 inches of snow and sleet. This combined with wind gusts in excess of 40 mph created blizzard conditions across much of northwest Ohio.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Blizzard conditions were experienced during the late afternoon and evening hours of February 1st as an intense low pressure system approached from the southwest. Wind gusts in excess of 40 mph, combined with the falling heavy snow and sleet, created reduced visibilities below an eighth of a mile at times. Snowfall rates of 1 to 2 inches per hour were common, with totals across the county generally ranging between 4 and 7 inches. Snow drifts of 2 to 3 feet were also reported across the county. The significant blowing and drifting snow resulted in numerous accidents and school closings across the region.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure moved up the Ohio Valley on January 11th and crossed northern Ohio during the evening hours.  Snow associated with this low began around midday on the 11th with periods of moderate to heavy snow reported during the afternoon.  The snow lessened for a time during the evening but picked up again after midnight on the 12th as cold northerly winds behind the low began to interact with Lake Erie.  More moderate to heavy snow occurred during the early morning hours of the 12th.  The snow showers finally tapered to flurries late in the afternoon.  Snowfall totals across Cuyahoga, Summit and Geauga Counties ranged from 6 to 12 inches.  Peak totals for these counties included: 15.4 inches at Sagamore Hills in Summit County; 14.0 inches in Russell Township in Geauga County and 12.7 inches at Broadview Heights in Cuyahoga County.  Northwest winds gusted to as much as 25 mph during this event and caused considerable blowing and drifting.  Travel was very difficult during this event.  Many school districts were either closed or delayed during this storm.  Many accidents were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface low pressure system, intially analyzed in east Texas on January 31st moved northeast over the Ohio Valley to western Pennsylvania by Wednesday morning, Febraury 2nd. Initial precipitation ahead of the warm front began as light snow before changing over to sleet and then freezing rain early on Tuesday Feb 1st. A break in the mixed precipitation occurred during the day on Tuesday with light rain and drizzle occuring for Pittsburgh and areas south while the counties north of I-80 continued to have snow. Tuesday evening, as the low pressure system approached the area another round of heavy precipitation moved over the region with the counties north of I-80 changing from snow to freezing rain. To the south, precipitation remained rain. Ice accumulations on the 1st ranged from one quarter to more than one half inch over eastern Ohio, western Pennsylvania and Garrett county Maryland. The counties north of I-80 generally had snow amounts between 4 and 6 inches with Venango county measuring 6.0 inches of snow in 6 hours. The weight of the ice caused downed trees and power outages through Carroll, Columbiana, Tuscarawas and Mercer counties.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure developed over the southern Plains on February 1st and then moved quickly northeast.  This low moved across northern Ohio during the morning hours of February 2nd.  Three waves of wintry precipitation were associated with this storm across northern Ohio.  The first wave of precipitation moved into Northwest Ohio during the late evening hours of January 31st.  This precipitation overspread the remainder of northern Ohio during the early morning hours of the 1st.  Locations south a line from near Marion to Mansfield to Warren in Trumbull County saw a mixture of freezing rain and sleet.  Widespread ice accumulations of over a quarter inch were reported in this area with up to a half inch of ice accumulation in portions of Stark, Holmes and Mahoning Counties.  Locations further north saw a four to six hour period of moderate to heavy snow.  Snow accumulations from the Toledo area eastward across the Cleveland metropolitan area and into Northeast Ohio were between four and six inches with a few reports of up to seven inches.  Some sleet was also reported.  This first wave of precipitation exited Ohio to the east around daybreak on the 1st.  Most of the daylight hours of the 1st were quiet with just some light snow and patchy freezing drizzle reported.  The second wave of precipitation began during the late afternoon and evening hours.  Most of this was also mixed freezing rain and sleet.  Only the northwestern corner of the area saw predominantly snow.  Ice accumulations of between one quarter and one half inch were reported along with an inch or more of sleet accumulation.  This mixture coated area roads and streets and resulted in treacherous travel.  A few power outages resulted from the freezing rain early on the 1st.  Power outages were more numerous on 2nd.  Trees and power lines already weighted down from ice from the 1st gave way with this second round of ice.  Stark County was especially hard hit by power outages.  Over 50,000 residents in the Canton area alone lost power.  It took several days for power to be fully restored.  Wrap around snow behind the low affected northern Ohio during the daylight hours of the 2nd.  Some of the snow was locally heavy with visibilities at times less than a quarter mile.  Much of the area saw between one and three inches of snow.  The snow quickly ended from west to east during the evening hours.  Strong winds also accompanied the precipitation.  Winds were initially from the northeast and gusty.  Gusts up to 35 mph were reported near the Lake Erie shoreline.  Winds became southerly right ahead of the low and then became southwest to west as the low exited to the east.  Wind gusts as much as 40 mph occurred during the morning hours of the 2nd with speeds gradually subsiding during the afternoon.  A lot of blowing and drifting snow was reported.  Over 500 schools were closed in northern Ohio on February 1st and 2nd.  Many schools remained closed on February 3rd because of residual thick ice on parking lots, sidewalks and secondary roads.  This ice resulted from the combination of two days of mixed freezing rain, sleet and snow and was up to two inches thick in some areas.  Removing the ice from sidewalks and driveways was nearly impossible.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three waves of wintry precipitation were associated with this storm across northern Ohio. Snow began in Medina County around midnight on February 1st. A four to six hour period of moderate to heavy snow occurred with accumulations of 3 to 5 inches.  Most of the daylight hours of the 1st were quiet with just some light snow and patchy freezing drizzle reported. The second wave of precipitation began during the late afternoon and evening hours. Most of this was mixed freezing rain and sleet. Ice accumulations of over a quarter inch along with an inch or more of sleet accumulation occurred. This mixture coated area roads and streets and resulted in treacherous travel. A few power outages were reported on 2nd.  Wrap around snow behind the low affected northern Ohio during the daylight hours of the 2nd.  Much of the area saw between one and three inches of snow.  Many schools were closed on the 1st and 2nd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure tracked slowly east across the Ohio Valley on February 20th and 21st.  A warm front stretching east from this low moved north into northern Ohio on the 20th and caused freezing rain to develop from west to east during the evening hours.  The freezing rain continued for several hours and then ended just before daybreak on the 21st.  Ice accumulations of between one quarter and one third of an inch were reported from Lorain County eastward across the Cleveland metropolitan area and into Lake, Geauga and Trumbull Counties.  The freezing rain was much lighter further south and west.  Colder temperatures were reported in Ashtabula County which allowed most of the precipitation to fall as snow and resulted in little if any ice accumulation.  At the peak of the storm, over 80,000 people were without power in northern Ohio.  Mass transit systems in Cuyahoga County were briefly shut down because of power outages.  It took nearly a full day for the power outages to be completely restored.  Many schools not already closed for the President's Day Holiday either canceled or delayed classes on the 21st.  A second wave of precipitation affected northern Ohio during the afternoon and evening hours of the 21st.  This second round of precipitation fell entirely as snow.  The snow was heaviest along and north of the U.S. Highway 30 corridor with four to six hours of moderate to heavy snow reported.  Snowfall rates at times exceeded two inches per hour with visibilities near zero.  Over six inches of snow fell from Crawford County eastward across the Mansfield, Wooster, Canton and Youngstown areas.  The heavy snow also extended north into Medina, Summit and Portage Counties.  Accumulations of eight or more inches were reported in portions of northern Richland, Ashland and Wayne Counties.  The heaviest snow fell during the evening commute and travel was nearly impossible at times.  Motorists reported their commute times were several times normal.  Interstate 76 in Summit and Medina Counties was closed for nearly six hours after numerous accidents occurred between 5 and 6 pm.  Hundreds of motorists were stranded in their cars.   It took most of the night for road crews to clean the area roads up.  Several local officials reported that conditions during the peak of the storm were the worst they had seen in years.  Many school districts remained closed on the 22nd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure tracked slowly east across the Ohio Valley on February 20th and 21st. Snow associated with this system spread into Ashland County during the afternoon hours of the 21st.  Four to six hours of moderate to heavy snow occurred from the middle portion of the afternoon through early evening.  Snowfall rates at times exceeded two inches per hour with visibilities near zero. Six to eight inches of snow fell over much of Ashland County.  It took most of the night for road crews to clean the area roads up. Several local officials reported that conditions during the peak of the storm were the worst they had seen in years. Many school districts were closed on the 22nd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deepening low pressure system, originating from the southern plains, moved towards the western Great Lakes on Wednesday March 9th. A wintry mix of precipitation changed over to all rain as the warm front associated with this system pushed across the region on Wednesday morning. Periods of moderate to heavy rain continued through the passage of the upper level trough late Thursday. With much of the region already saturated from previous rainfall over the past 2 weeks many streams and creeks were already elevated. The additional 1 to 2 inches of rainfall resulted in my streams and creeks gong out of their banks throughout east central Ohio, western Pennsylvania and northern West Virginia. As the low progressed to the east rain changed back to snow bringing 4 to 5 inches of heavy wet snow to east central Ohio and northwestern Pennsylvania, mainly north of I-70. Higher amounts of 6 to 8 inches fell in in Columbiana, Mercer, Lawrence and Venango counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement and the county emergency manager reports that Conotton Creek is out of its banks and flooding State Route 151 in Scio and several secondary roads in Conotton.

Over six inches of snow fell from Crawford County eastward across the Mansfield, Wooster, Canton and Youngstown areas.  The heavy snow also extended north into Medina, Summit and Portage Counties.  Accumulations of eight or more inches were reported in portions of northern Richland, Ashland and Wayne Counties.  The heaviest snow fell during the evening commute and travel was nearly impossible at times.  Motorists reported their commute times were several times normal.  Interstate 76 in Summit and Medina Counties was closed for nearly six hours after numerous accidents occurred between 5 and 6 pm.  Hundreds of motorists were stranded in their cars.   It took most of the night for road crews to clean the area roads up.  Several local officials reported that conditions during the peak of the storm were the worst they had seen in years.  Many school districts remained closed on the 22nd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure in the lower Mississippi Valley developed during the evening of Monday, January 10th and tracked into the Ohio Valley overnight. Early on Tuesday this low strengthened as it crossed the region into the Mid Atlantic.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Police in Greenville measured 5.5 inches of snow. A spotter in Bradford measured 4.2 inches, while the cooperative observer in Versailles measured 4 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure developed over the southern Plains on February 1st and then moved quickly northeast.  This low moved across northern Ohio during the morning hours of February 2nd.  Three waves of wintry precipitation were associated with this storm across northern Ohio.  The first wave of precipitation moved into Northwest Ohio during the late evening hours of January 31st.  This precipitation overspread the remainder of northern Ohio during the early morning hours of the 1st.  Locations south a line from near Marion to Mansfield to Warren in Trumbull County saw a mixture of freezing rain and sleet.  Widespread ice accumulations of over a quarter inch were reported in this area with up to a half inch of ice accumulation in portions of Stark, Holmes and Mahoning Counties.  Locations further north saw a four to six hour period of moderate to heavy snow.  Snow accumulations from the Toledo area eastward across the Cleveland metropolitan area and into Northeast Ohio were between four and six inches with a few reports of up to seven inches.  Some sleet was also reported.  This first wave of precipitation exited Ohio to the east around daybreak on the 1st.  Most of the daylight hours of the 1st were quiet with just some light snow and patchy freezing drizzle reported.  The second wave of precipitation began during the late afternoon and evening hours.  Most of this was also mixed freezing rain and sleet.  Only the northwestern corner of the area saw predominantly snow.  Ice accumulations of between one quarter and one half inch were reported along with an inch or more of sleet accumulation.  This mixture coated area roads and streets and resulted in treacherous travel.  A few power outages resulted from the freezing rain early on the 1st.  Power outages were more numerous on 2nd.  Trees and power lines already weighted down from ice from the 1st gave way with this second round of ice.  Stark County was especially hard hit by power outages.  Over 50,000 residents in the Canton area alone lost power.  It took several days for power to be fully restored.  Wrap around snow behind the low affected northern Ohio during the daylight hours of the 2nd.  Some of the snow was locally heavy with visibilities at times less than a quarter mile.  Much of the area saw between one and three inches of snow.  The snow quickly ended from west to east during the evening hours.  Strong winds also accompanied the precipitation.  Winds were initially from the northeast and gusty.  Gusts up to 35 mph were reported near the Lake Erie shoreline.  Winds became southerly right ahead of the low and then became southwest to west as the low exited to the east.  Wind gusts as much as 40 mph occurred during the morning hours of the 2nd with speeds gradually subsiding during the afternoon.  A lot of blowing and drifting snow was reported.  Over 500 schools were closed in northern Ohio on February 1st and 2nd.  Many schools remained closed on February 3rd because of residual thick ice on parking lots, sidewalks and secondary roads.  This ice resulted from the combination of two days of mixed freezing rain, sleet and snow and was up to two inches thick in some areas.  Removing the ice from sidewalks and driveways was nearly impossible.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mixture of freezing rain and sleet fell on Holmes County during the early morning hours of February 1st.  More than a quarter inch of ice accumulation was reported by daybreak.  A second round of freezing rain and drizzle began during the afternoon hours and continued into the early morning hours of the 2nd.  Some sleet mixed with the freezing rain at times.  A few power outages resulted from the freezing rain early on the 1st.  Power outages were more numerous on 2nd.  Wrap around snow behind the low affected northern Ohio during the daylight hours of the 2nd. Some of the snow was locally heavy with visibilities at times less than a quarter mile. Much of the area saw between one and three inches of snow. The snow quickly ended from west to east during the evening hours. Strong winds also accompanied the precipitation.   Many schools were closed on the 1st, 2nd and 3rd because of residual thick ice on parking lots, sidewalks and secondary roads. This ice resulted from the combination of two days of mixed freezing rain, sleet and snow and was up to two inches thick in some areas. Removing the ice from sidewalks and driveways was nearly impossible.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms developed north of a warm frontal boundary stretched across central Ohio on March 23rd. The center of low pressure was located over the Chicago, Illinois area at 0700 local time and tracked east-southeast across Indiana and Ohio during the morning and afternoon hours. A tornado watch was issued early that afternoon and a winter weather advisory was issued a couple of hours later in anticipation of freezing rain later that evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm spotters reported nickle sized hail lasting 1 to 2 minutes along Route 20.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deepening low pressure system, originating from the southern plains, moved towards the western Great Lakes on Wednesday March 9th. A wintry mix of precipitation changed over to all rain as the warm front associated with this system pushed across the region on Wednesday morning. Periods of moderate to heavy rain continued through the passage of the upper level trough late Thursday. With much of the region already saturated from previous rainfall over the past 2 weeks many streams and creeks were already elevated. The additional 1 to 2 inches of rainfall resulted in my streams and creeks gong out of their banks throughout east central Ohio, western Pennsylvania and northern West Virginia. As the low progressed to the east rain changed back to snow bringing 4 to 5 inches of heavy wet snow to east central Ohio and northwestern Pennsylvania, mainly north of I-70. Higher amounts of 6 to 8 inches fell in in Columbiana, Mercer, Lawrence and Venango counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement and the county emergency manager reports numerous roads closed throughout Carroll county including State Routes 39, 332, 212 in Dellroy, Carrollton, and Sherrodsville respectively. Alot of the roads around Leesville lake were closed due to flooding of the streams going into the lake.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface low pressure system, intially analyzed in east Texas on January 31st moved northeast over the Ohio Valley to western Pennsylvania by Wednesday morning, Febraury 2nd. Initial precipitation ahead of the warm front began as light snow before changing over to sleet and then freezing rain early on Tuesday Feb 1st. A break in the mixed precipitation occurred during the day on Tuesday with light rain and drizzle occuring for Pittsburgh and areas south while the counties north of I-80 continued to have snow. Tuesday evening, as the low pressure system approached the area another round of heavy precipitation moved over the region with the counties north of I-80 changing from snow to freezing rain. To the south, precipitation remained rain. Ice accumulations on the 1st ranged from one quarter to more than one half inch over eastern Ohio, western Pennsylvania and Garrett county Maryland. The counties north of I-80 generally had snow amounts between 4 and 6 inches with Venango county measuring 6.0 inches of snow in 6 hours. The weight of the ice caused downed trees and power outages through Carroll, Columbiana, Tuscarawas and Mercer counties.

As the low progressed to the east rain changed back to snow bringing 4 to 5 inches of heavy wet snow to east central Ohio and northwestern Pennsylvania, mainly north of I-70. Higher amounts of 6 to 8 inches fell in in Columbiana, Mercer, Lawrence and Venango counties.

An area of low pressure developed over the southern Plains on February 1st and then moved quickly northeast.  This low moved across northern Ohio during the morning hours of February 2nd.  Three waves of wintry precipitation were associated with this storm across northern Ohio.  The first wave of precipitation moved into Northwest Ohio during the late evening hours of January 31st.  This precipitation overspread the remainder of northern Ohio during the early morning hours of the 1st.  Locations south a line from near Marion to Mansfield to Warren in Trumbull County saw a mixture of freezing rain and sleet.  Widespread ice accumulations of over a quarter inch were reported in this area with up to a half inch of ice accumulation in portions of Stark, Holmes and Mahoning Counties.  Locations further north saw a four to six hour period of moderate to heavy snow.  Snow accumulations from the Toledo area eastward across the Cleveland metropolitan area and into Northeast Ohio were between four and six inches with a few reports of up to seven inches.  Some sleet was also reported.  This first wave of precipitation exited Ohio to the east around daybreak on the 1st.  Most of the daylight hours of the 1st were quiet with just some light snow and patchy freezing drizzle reported.  The second wave of precipitation began during the late afternoon and evening hours.  Most of this was also mixed freezing rain and sleet.  Only the northwestern corner of the area saw predominantly snow.  Ice accumulations of between one quarter and one half inch were reported along with an inch or more of sleet accumulation.  This mixture coated area roads and streets and resulted in treacherous travel.  A few power outages resulted from the freezing rain early on the 1st.  Power outages were more numerous on 2nd.  Trees and power lines already weighted down from ice from the 1st gave way with this second round of ice.  Stark County was especially hard hit by power outages.  Over 50,000 residents in the Canton area alone lost power.  It took several days for power to be fully restored.  Wrap around snow behind the low affected northern Ohio during the daylight hours of the 2nd.  Some of the snow was locally heavy with visibilities at times less than a quarter mile.  Much of the area saw between one and three inches of snow.  The snow quickly ended from west to east during the evening hours.  Strong winds also accompanied the precipitation.  Winds were initially from the northeast and gusty.  Gusts up to 35 mph were reported near the Lake Erie shoreline.  Winds became southerly right ahead of the low and then became southwest to west as the low exited to the east.  Wind gusts as much as 40 mph occurred during the morning hours of the 2nd with speeds gradually subsiding during the afternoon.  A lot of blowing and drifting snow was reported.  Over 500 schools were closed in northern Ohio on February 1st and 2nd.  Many schools remained closed on February 3rd because of residual thick ice on parking lots, sidewalks and secondary roads.  This ice resulted from the combination of two days of mixed freezing rain, sleet and snow and was up to two inches thick in some areas.  Removing the ice from sidewalks and driveways was nearly impossible.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface low pressure system, intially analyzed in east Texas on January 31st moved northeast over the Ohio Valley to western Pennsylvania by Wednesday morning, Febraury 2nd. Initial precipitation ahead of the warm front began as light snow before changing over to sleet and then freezing rain early on Tuesday Feb 1st. A break in the mixed precipitation occurred during the day on Tuesday with light rain and drizzle occuring for Pittsburgh and areas south while the counties north of I-80 continued to have snow. Tuesday evening, as the low pressure system approached the area another round of heavy precipitation moved over the region with the counties north of I-80 changing from snow to freezing rain. To the south, precipitation remained rain. Ice accumulations on the 1st ranged from one quarter to more than one half inch over eastern Ohio, western Pennsylvania and Garrett county Maryland. The counties north of I-80 generally had snow amounts between 4 and 6 inches with Venango county measuring 6.0 inches of snow in 6 hours. The weight of the ice caused downed trees and power outages through Carroll, Columbiana, Tuscarawas and Mercer counties.

Showers and thunderstorms developed north of a warm frontal boundary stretched across central Ohio on March 23rd. The center of low pressure was located over the Chicago, Illinois area at 0700 local time and tracked east-southeast across Indiana and Ohio during the morning and afternoon hours. A tornado watch was issued early that afternoon and a winter weather advisory was issued a couple of hours later in anticipation of freezing rain later that evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spotters reported hail lasting for five minutes. Quarter sized hail was mixed in with penny sized hail during the event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface low pressure system, intially analyzed in east Texas on January 31st moved northeast over the Ohio Valley to western Pennsylvania by Wednesday morning, Febraury 2nd. Initial precipitation ahead of the warm front began as light snow before changing over to sleet and then freezing rain early on Tuesday Feb 1st. A break in the mixed precipitation occurred during the day on Tuesday with light rain and drizzle occuring for Pittsburgh and areas south while the counties north of I-80 continued to have snow. Tuesday evening, as the low pressure system approached the area another round of heavy precipitation moved over the region with the counties north of I-80 changing from snow to freezing rain. To the south, precipitation remained rain. Ice accumulations on the 1st ranged from one quarter to more than one half inch over eastern Ohio, western Pennsylvania and Garrett county Maryland. The counties north of I-80 generally had snow amounts between 4 and 6 inches with Venango county measuring 6.0 inches of snow in 6 hours. The weight of the ice caused downed trees and power outages through Carroll, Columbiana, Tuscarawas and Mercer counties.

A surface low pressure system, intially analyzed in east Texas on January 31st moved northeast over the Ohio Valley to western Pennsylvania by Wednesday morning, Febraury 2nd. Initial precipitation ahead of the warm front began as light snow before changing over to sleet and then freezing rain early on Tuesday Feb 1st. A break in the mixed precipitation occurred during the day on Tuesday with light rain and drizzle occuring for Pittsburgh and areas south while the counties north of I-80 continued to have snow. Tuesday evening, as the low pressure system approached the area another round of heavy precipitation moved over the region with the counties north of I-80 changing from snow to freezing rain. To the south, precipitation remained rain. Ice accumulations on the 1st ranged from one quarter to more than one half inch over eastern Ohio, western Pennsylvania and Garrett county Maryland. The counties north of I-80 generally had snow amounts between 4 and 6 inches with Venango county measuring 6.0 inches of snow in 6 hours. The weight of the ice caused downed trees and power outages through Carroll, Columbiana, Tuscarawas and Mercer counties.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure developed over the southern Plains on February 1st and then moved quickly northeast.  This low moved across northern Ohio during the morning hours of February 2nd.  Three waves of wintry precipitation were associated with this storm across northern Ohio.  The first wave of precipitation moved into Northwest Ohio during the late evening hours of January 31st.  This precipitation overspread the remainder of northern Ohio during the early morning hours of the 1st.  Locations south a line from near Marion to Mansfield to Warren in Trumbull County saw a mixture of freezing rain and sleet.  Widespread ice accumulations of over a quarter inch were reported in this area with up to a half inch of ice accumulation in portions of Stark, Holmes and Mahoning Counties.  Locations further north saw a four to six hour period of moderate to heavy snow.  Snow accumulations from the Toledo area eastward across the Cleveland metropolitan area and into Northeast Ohio were between four and six inches with a few reports of up to seven inches.  Some sleet was also reported.  This first wave of precipitation exited Ohio to the east around daybreak on the 1st.  Most of the daylight hours of the 1st were quiet with just some light snow and patchy freezing drizzle reported.  The second wave of precipitation began during the late afternoon and evening hours.  Most of this was also mixed freezing rain and sleet.  Only the northwestern corner of the area saw predominantly snow.  Ice accumulations of between one quarter and one half inch were reported along with an inch or more of sleet accumulation.  This mixture coated area roads and streets and resulted in treacherous travel.  A few power outages resulted from the freezing rain early on the 1st.  Power outages were more numerous on 2nd.  Trees and power lines already weighted down from ice from the 1st gave way with this second round of ice.  Stark County was especially hard hit by power outages.  Over 50,000 residents in the Canton area alone lost power.  It took several days for power to be fully restored.  Wrap around snow behind the low affected northern Ohio during the daylight hours of the 2nd.  Some of the snow was locally heavy with visibilities at times less than a quarter mile.  Much of the area saw between one and three inches of snow.  The snow quickly ended from west to east during the evening hours.  Strong winds also accompanied the precipitation.  Winds were initially from the northeast and gusty.  Gusts up to 35 mph were reported near the Lake Erie shoreline.  Winds became southerly right ahead of the low and then became southwest to west as the low exited to the east.  Wind gusts as much as 40 mph occurred during the morning hours of the 2nd with speeds gradually subsiding during the afternoon.  A lot of blowing and drifting snow was reported.  Over 500 schools were closed in northern Ohio on February 1st and 2nd.  Many schools remained closed on February 3rd because of residual thick ice on parking lots, sidewalks and secondary roads.  This ice resulted from the combination of two days of mixed freezing rain, sleet and snow and was up to two inches thick in some areas.  Removing the ice from sidewalks and driveways was nearly impossible.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three waves of wintry precipitation were associated with this storm across northern Ohio. Snow began in Erie County around midnight on February 1st. A four to six hour period of moderate to heavy snow occurred with accumulations of 3 to 5 inches.  Most of the daylight hours of the 1st were quiet with just some flurries reported. The second wave of precipitation began during the late afternoon and evening hours. This precipitation started as a mixture of snow and sleet but changed to all snow during the middle part of the evening.  Some heavy snow was reported during the late evening hours.  The snow changed to freezing rain around 4 am and finally ended around daybreak after about a tenth of an inch of ice accumulation.  The mixture of snow, freezing rain and sleet coated area roads and streets and resulted in treacherous travel.  Wrap around snow behind the low affected northern Ohio during the daylight hours of the 2nd.  Much of the area saw between one and three inches of snow.   Snow totals on the 1st and 2nd ranged from 6 to 8 inches over the county.  Many schools were closed on the 1st and 2nd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure tracked slowly east across the Ohio Valley on February 20th and 21st.  A warm front stretching east from this low moved north into northern Ohio on the 20th and caused freezing rain to develop from west to east during the evening hours.  The freezing rain continued for several hours and then ended just before daybreak on the 21st.  Ice accumulations of between one quarter and one third of an inch were reported from Lorain County eastward across the Cleveland metropolitan area and into Lake, Geauga and Trumbull Counties.  The freezing rain was much lighter further south and west.  Colder temperatures were reported in Ashtabula County which allowed most of the precipitation to fall as snow and resulted in little if any ice accumulation.  At the peak of the storm, over 80,000 people were without power in northern Ohio.  Mass transit systems in Cuyahoga County were briefly shut down because of power outages.  It took nearly a full day for the power outages to be completely restored.  Many schools not already closed for the President's Day Holiday either canceled or delayed classes on the 21st.  A second wave of precipitation affected northern Ohio during the afternoon and evening hours of the 21st.  This second round of precipitation fell entirely as snow.  The snow was heaviest along and north of the U.S. Highway 30 corridor with four to six hours of moderate to heavy snow reported.  Snowfall rates at times exceeded two inches per hour with visibilities near zero.  Over six inches of snow fell from Crawford County eastward across the Mansfield, Wooster, Canton and Youngstown areas.  The heavy snow also extended north into Medina, Summit and Portage Counties.  Accumulations of eight or more inches were reported in portions of northern Richland, Ashland and Wayne Counties.  The heaviest snow fell during the evening commute and travel was nearly impossible at times.  Motorists reported their commute times were several times normal.  Interstate 76 in Summit and Medina Counties was closed for nearly six hours after numerous accidents occurred between 5 and 6 pm.  Hundreds of motorists were stranded in their cars.   It took most of the night for road crews to clean the area roads up.  Several local officials reported that conditions during the peak of the storm were the worst they had seen in years.  Many school districts remained closed on the 22nd.

At the peak of the storm, over 80,000 people were without power in northern Ohio. It took nearly a full day for the power outages to be completely restored. The heaviest snow fell during the evening commute and travel was nearly impossible at times.  Motorists reported their commute times were several times normal.  Interstate 76 in Summit and Medina Counties was closed for nearly six hours after numerous accidents occurred between 5 and 6 pm.  Hundreds of motorists were stranded in their cars.   It took most of the night for road crews to clean the area roads up.  Several local officials reported that conditions during the peak of the storm were the worst they had seen in years.  Many school districts remained closed on the 22nd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure tracked slowly east across the Ohio Valley on February 20th and 21st. A warm front stretching east from this low moved north into northern Ohio on the 20th and caused freezing rain to develop from west to east during the evening hours. The freezing rain continued for several hours and then ended just before daybreak on the 21st. Ice accumulations of between one quarter and one third of an inch were reported in Lake County. At the peak of the storm, over 80,000 people were without power in northern Ohio. It took nearly a full day for the power outages to be completely restored. Many schools not already closed for the President's Day Holiday either canceled or delayed classes on the 21st.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of severe storms moved through during the morning hours of February 28th.  The main threats were damaging winds and flash flooding, however one tornado also occurred with this event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado touched down south of Millersport near the intersection of Millersport Road and Canal Road where tree damage was observed.  The tornado then continued to the east and directly hit a farm on the north side of Canal Road.  At this location, metal grain bins were tossed and rolled and trees were uprooted.  A masonry wall was also flattened and numerous objects were impaled into the ground as well.  A newly constructed barn had the roof removed and numerous pieces of sheet metal were strewn into adjoining fields.  The tornado then continued on and hit two residences on Lake Road.  A modular home had the roof completely removed and tossed and trees were uprooted.  A barn was completely destroyed in the area as well.  At another residence, one of the exterior walls was buckled from the wind blowing the door of the home open.  Corn debris from a nearby field was driven into the ground.  A stick was found driven into the siding on the front of the home.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure developed over the southern Plains on February 1st and then moved quickly northeast.  This low moved across northern Ohio during the morning hours of February 2nd.  Three waves of wintry precipitation were associated with this storm across northern Ohio.  The first wave of precipitation moved into Northwest Ohio during the late evening hours of January 31st.  This precipitation overspread the remainder of northern Ohio during the early morning hours of the 1st.  Locations south a line from near Marion to Mansfield to Warren in Trumbull County saw a mixture of freezing rain and sleet.  Widespread ice accumulations of over a quarter inch were reported in this area with up to a half inch of ice accumulation in portions of Stark, Holmes and Mahoning Counties.  Locations further north saw a four to six hour period of moderate to heavy snow.  Snow accumulations from the Toledo area eastward across the Cleveland metropolitan area and into Northeast Ohio were between four and six inches with a few reports of up to seven inches.  Some sleet was also reported.  This first wave of precipitation exited Ohio to the east around daybreak on the 1st.  Most of the daylight hours of the 1st were quiet with just some light snow and patchy freezing drizzle reported.  The second wave of precipitation began during the late afternoon and evening hours.  Most of this was also mixed freezing rain and sleet.  Only the northwestern corner of the area saw predominantly snow.  Ice accumulations of between one quarter and one half inch were reported along with an inch or more of sleet accumulation.  This mixture coated area roads and streets and resulted in treacherous travel.  A few power outages resulted from the freezing rain early on the 1st.  Power outages were more numerous on 2nd.  Trees and power lines already weighted down from ice from the 1st gave way with this second round of ice.  Stark County was especially hard hit by power outages.  Over 50,000 residents in the Canton area alone lost power.  It took several days for power to be fully restored.  Wrap around snow behind the low affected northern Ohio during the daylight hours of the 2nd.  Some of the snow was locally heavy with visibilities at times less than a quarter mile.  Much of the area saw between one and three inches of snow.  The snow quickly ended from west to east during the evening hours.  Strong winds also accompanied the precipitation.  Winds were initially from the northeast and gusty.  Gusts up to 35 mph were reported near the Lake Erie shoreline.  Winds became southerly right ahead of the low and then became southwest to west as the low exited to the east.  Wind gusts as much as 40 mph occurred during the morning hours of the 2nd with speeds gradually subsiding during the afternoon.  A lot of blowing and drifting snow was reported.  Over 500 schools were closed in northern Ohio on February 1st and 2nd.  Many schools remained closed on February 3rd because of residual thick ice on parking lots, sidewalks and secondary roads.  This ice resulted from the combination of two days of mixed freezing rain, sleet and snow and was up to two inches thick in some areas.  Removing the ice from sidewalks and driveways was nearly impossible.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three waves of wintry precipitation were associated with this storm across northern Ohio. Snow began in Seneca County around midnight on February 1st. A four to six hour period of moderate to heavy snow occurred with accumulations of 3 to 5 inches.  Most of the daylight hours of the 1st were quiet with just some flurries reported. The second wave of precipitation began during the late afternoon and evening hours. This precipitation started as a mixture of snow and sleet but changed to all snow during the middle part of the evening.  Some heavy snow was reported during the late evening hours.  The snow changed to freezing rain around 4 am and finally ended around daybreak after between one and two tenths of an inch of ice accumulation.  The mixture of snow, freezing rain and sleet coated area roads and streets and resulted in treacherous travel.  Wrap around snow behind the low affected northern Ohio during the daylight hours of the 2nd.  Much of the area saw between one and three inches of snow.   Snow totals on the 1st and 2nd ranged from 4 to 8 inches over the county.  Many schools were closed on the 1st and 2nd.

Showers and thunderstorms entered northwest Ohio during the late afternoon hours on the 29th. A frontal boundary oriented west-east along the southern Great Lakes aided in the development of the severe weather. High dew points, warm temperatures, and high instability along and near the frontal boundary also supported the development of severe thunderstorms which tracked east across northern Ohio for several hours that evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds downed several large trees.

During the early morning hours of May 23, an area of low pressure entered the Upper Great Lakes Region. Substantial amounts of warm air and deep moisture entered northern Ohio from the southern United States. That instability lead to widespread severe weather, which began early in the afternoon in the western portions of the county warning area and persisted late into the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds downed several large tree limbs in Weston. As the severe thunderstorm tracked east, several large trees were downed in the Bowling Green area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system west of the Ohio Valley approached during peak heating on May 25th. This resulted in the development of widespread severe thunderstorms with large hail and damaging winds during the afternoon and evening hours. Later in the evening, a quasi-linear convective system swept west to east across the area. The result was straight line wind damage and a few tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A National Weather Service storm survey confirmed that a tornado touched down three miles west of Bloomingburg. Near the beginning of the path one barn was damaged and another barn was destroyed.  Further along the path another barn was damaged due to the tornado.  The estimated maximum wind speed based on damage was eighty miles per hour.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced a large swath of hail ranging in size from nickels to golf balls. Locations across Lorain county, including Vermilion, Elyria, and North Ridgeville were hit hardest.  Many vehicles and some homes were damaged by the hail.
