The snow melt and the rain combined to create widespread flooding.  Rapid rises in area streams and rivers occurred with near record crests eventually reported at some river locations.  Some flash flooding was also reported.  A woman was killed near Norwalk after her car was swept off of the road by flood waters.  Dozens of water rescues were reported and hundreds of people had to be evacuated from their homes.  Dozens of roads had be closed because of flooding and washouts were reported in several counties.  Over a thousand homes were damaged by flooding.  Damage estimates from northern Ohio topped $30 million for this event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain and rapid snow melt led to a rapid rise in the Tuscarawas River and the Nimishillen Creek. The Nimishillen Creek reached a 100 year flood stage and it's second highest crest on record of 12.96 feet.  Rainfall observations across the county ranged from 1.5-2 which fell mainly during the morning of the 28th. Six to ten inches of heavy snow was on the ground at the onset of the rain.  This snow rapidly melted as temperatures warmed into the 50s on the 28th.  Up to an additional inch of water equivalent rainfall was released from the melted snow.   Reports that 40 to 50 people people had be rescued from homes and vehicles as the water came up.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As a low pressure system tracked along a warm front across Ohio and Pennsylvania severe thunderstorms developed just south of the front in Ohio and progressed eastward. Large hail was reported with many of the storms, with hail up to the size of golf balls reported in both Ohio and Pennsylvania. A strong supercell thunderstorm produced an EF-2 tornado that crossed through Westmoreland county in Pennsylvania during rush hour. Around 90 homes sustained damage in Westmoreland county.
A line of storms moved through the area during the late night hours of April 19th and into the early morning hours of April 20th.
A very unstable airmass was in place south of a warm front in the area. This unstable airmass led to the development of widespread severe weather.
A line of storms moved through the area during the late night hours of April 19th and into the early morning hours of April 20th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were reported down due to damaging thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered severe thunderstorms developed well ahead of a cold front in an unstable airmass.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency management reported trees down south of Mechanicstown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very unstable airmass was in place south of a warm front in the area. This unstable airmass led to the development of widespread severe weather.
A slow moving cold front moving southeast across the Upper Ohio valley produced scattered severe thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported trees down near Shadyside.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An approaching cold front combined with moderate instability and high shear to produce severe thunderstorms across central and southern Ohio during the late afternoon and evening hours. The main threats from these storms were large hail, damaging thunderstorm winds, and locally heavy rain.
A very unstable airmass was in place south of a warm front in the area. This unstable airmass led to the development of widespread severe weather.

During the evening and nighttime hours of May 11th, an area of low pressure located over southern Wisconsin slowly progressed northeast over the upper Great Lakes. A frontal boundary extended southeast of the low, across portions of Indiana into southern Ohio. That frontal boundary lifted north during the day on May 12 and stalled directly over northern Ohio. Thunderstorms developed along this boundary and produced large hail and significant rainfall throughout the day on May 12th. The severe thunderstorms and flash flooding continued through May 13th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced quarter sized hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved across northern Ohio on August 1st.  Showers and thunderstorms developed along and ahead of this front.  A few of the stronger storms became severe with large hail reported at several locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter sized hail was observed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary boundary remained draped across the upper Ohio Valley on July 23rd.  Showers and thunderstorms developed in association with this front with a few of the stronger storms becoming severe.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds downed a few large tree limbs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the afternoon on the 23rd, a broken line of convection formed in southeast Ohio ahead of a slow moving cold front.  Dry air in the mid levels, as well as deep layer undirectional shear, lent toward a damaging wind threat as opposed to large hail. ||After the initial line passed well into West Virginia, recovering surface temperatures and enough shear was present to support further development during the early evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down along Pleasant Hill Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A quasi-linear convective system moved across an unstable airmass and produced widespread severe weather.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The wind gust was estimated by the spotter.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A quasi-linear convective system moved across an unstable airmass and produced widespread severe weather.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Panels were ripped off of a barn due to damaging thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A quasi-linear convective system moved across an unstable airmass and produced widespread severe weather.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees were reported down due to damaging thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front was located from southwestern Indiana into northwestern Ohio through the 25th and into the early morning hours of the 26th. Several waves moved along the front, with each one generating thunderstorms. Numerous hail reports were received, with pockets of wind damage as well as an isolated tornado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported power lines down across Agether Road, east of Kemp Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed along a trough axis in northern Indiana. These storms became severe before exiting Indiana and then moving south and southeast across western and south central parts of Ohio. One these severe storms moved into Northern Kentucky before weakening. The main severe weather threats from these storms were damaging winds and large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few trees were damaged about 20 to 30 feet above the ground due to damaging thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated severe thunderstorms developed ahead of a cold front moving across the upper Ohio Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reported power lines down in New Philadelphia.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of strong to severe thunderstorms moved from northern Indiana into northwestern Ohio, where extreme instability and moderate shear were in place. Damaging winds accompanied several of the storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement officials reported several trees blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved into Northern and Central Ohio during the day...then became stationary. Ground conditions were saturated from recent rainfall, so the additional storms and showers that developed along this boundary resulted in flooding. Some moist air from the Gulf of Mexico supplied sufficient moisture for rainfall rates over an inch per hour, which allowed for rapid runoff. Storm total rainfall amounts were generally an inch to an inch and a half along a line from Findlay, to Mansfield, and east to Youngstown. Though not a signficant amount of rain, it was sufficient to bring small streams and creeks out of their banks and lead to flooding of low lying and poor drainage areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Saturated ground conditions from recent rains set the stage for flooding on the 24th as a cold front moved into Stark County. Showers developed along this boundary, with rainfall amounts recorded at 0.80 inches in NE Canton and 0.50 inches in NW Canton. Though not a significant amount of rainfall, it became almost entirely runoff. The result was flooding of several roads, mainly in the Canton and Massillon Areas. Some roads flooded include RT21 from Lake to Cherry St. in Massillon along with Fire Station #4. Most of the rainfall fell within 30 minutes, so the rate of the rainfall contributed to the level of flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system combined with a slow moving cold front to produce widespread rain across the Ohio Valley. The result of the heavy rain was numerous reports of flooding in the area. Some of the flooding lingered into the morning hours of December 6.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High water due to heavy rain was reported to be causing some road closures in the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low situated over the north central United States allowed for the development of showers and isolated severe thunderstorms over Ohio.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reported trees and power lines down in Harlem Springs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the evening and nighttime hours of May 11th, an area of low pressure located over southern Wisconsin slowly progressed northeast over the upper Great Lakes. A frontal boundary extended southeast of the low, across portions of Indiana into southern Ohio. That frontal boundary lifted north during the day on May 12 and stalled directly over northern Ohio. Thunderstorms developed along this boundary and produced large hail and significant rainfall throughout the day on May 12th. The severe thunderstorms and flash flooding continued through May 13th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Torrential rainfall from strong thunderstorms brought as much as 1.5-2 to portions of Trumbull County. The rain fell at a high rate of over an inch per hour. This excessive rainfall caused water to runoff into streets as deep at 7-8 in the city of Leavittsburg.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave traveling north over a broad ridge in the central U.S dragged a weak cold front over the Ohio Valley. This coldfront set off thunderstorms in the afternoon and evening hours on the 18th. There were several reports of wind damage in eastern Ohio and western Pennsylvania with more than 11,000 people without power in western PA alone. In addition urban flooding and flash flooding occurred in various parts of Allegheny and Beaver counties. Cars were stranded in high water right around rush-hour on Washington Blvd in downtown Pittsburgh.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency management reports a tree down in Lore City.
